topic: A homeowner learns their house was a former crime scene
raw_output:
[story 1]

The realtor, a man whose smile seemed permanently plastered on, had assured me everything was pristine. "A beautiful fixer-upper, Mrs. Albright. Nothing to worry about." I gripped the edge of the antique mahogany desk, my knuckles white. I surveyed the living room, noting the undisturbed dust on the bookshelves, the perfectly aligned throw pillows. Yet, a cold knot tightened in my stomach. I’d been moving in for three weeks, and every time I walked past the hallway leading to the old study, a flicker of unease would surface. I decided to ignore it. It was just an old house settling, nothing more. I spent the afternoon painting the dining room a cheerful, blinding yellow, a deliberate act of forceful optimism designed to push the shadows back. When I finally turned off the power, the silence in the house felt heavy, expectant.

[story 2]

"It’s just an old building," Detective Miller had droned, his voice thick with practiced indifference. I leaned against the doorframe, arms crossed so tightly my biceps ached. I looked at the faint discoloration on the baseboard in the kitchen, a patch of darker, almost brownish staining that seemed out of place against the pristine white paint. I scoffed internally. People overreact. I had a life to get back to, a garden to tend, a mortgage to manage. I called the building inspector, a tedious exercise in bureaucratic reassurance. They confirmed the structure was sound, the plumbing functional. I walked into the master bedroom, deliberately pacing the perimeter of the room, my gaze sweeping over the window where the overgrown ivy clung. I forced myself to focus on the familiar scent of lemon polish, anything to drown out the unsettling feeling that something fundamental about this place refused to be ignored.

[story 3]

I traced the outline of a faint, almost invisible scratch on the hardwood floor in the upstairs landing. My jaw tightened. I had already spent a fortune on renovations, painstakingly restoring every fixture and refinishing every surface. To suggest this house held some grim history felt like an insult to the hard work I’d poured into making it *mine*. I dismissed the nagging feeling as stress, the residue of a long move. I went to the attic, determined to clear out the forgotten junk, hoping to sweep away any lingering unpleasantness. As I lifted a heavy, dusty trunk, I noticed a small, tarnished silver locket tucked beneath some yellowed linens. A sudden, sharp intake of breath caught in my throat. I shoved the locket into my pocket, a fierce resolve hardening my expression. This house belonged to me now, and whatever ghosts lingered, they would stay outside the perimeter of my control.
